elements.sent-2411-002: This paper proposes a Graph Neural Network-guided algorithm for solving word equations, based on the well-known Nielsen transformation for splitting equations.
elements.sent-2411-003: The algorithm iteratively rewrites the first terms of each side of an equation, giving rise to a tree-like search space.
elements.sent-2411-004: The choice of path at each split point of the tree significantly impacts solving time, motivating the use of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for efficient split decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-005: Split decisions are encoded as multi-classification tasks, and five graph representations of word equations are introduced to encode their structural information for GNNs.
elements.sent-2411-006: The algorithm is implemented as a solver named \OURSOLVER.
elements.sent-2411-007: Experiments are conducted on artificial and real-world benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-008: The algorithm performs particularly well on satisfiable problems.
elements.sent-2411-009: For single word equations , \OURSOLVER can solve significantly more problems than well-established string solvers.
elements.sent-2411-010: For the conjunction of multiple word equations, \OURSOLVER is competitive with state-of-the-art string solvers.
elements.sent-2411-011: Over the past few years, reasoning within specific theories, including arithmetic, arrays, or algebraic data structures, has become one the main challenges in automated reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-012: To address the needs of modern applications, new techniques have been developed, giving rise to SMT (Satisfiability Modulo Theories) solvers.
elements.sent-2411-013: SMT solvers implement efficient decision procedures and reasoning methods for a wide range of theories, and are used in applications such as verification.
elements.sent-2411-014: Among the theories supported by SMT solvers, the theory of strings has in particular received attention in the last years.
elements.sent-2411-015: Strings represent one of the most important data-types in programming, and string constraints are therefore relevant in various domains, from text processing to database management systems and web applications.
elements.sent-2411-016: One of the simplest kind of constraints supported by the SMT-LIB theory of strings~\cite{BarFT-RR-17 are word equations, i.e., equations in a free semigroup~\cite{Khm71 .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Makanin's work~\cite{Mak77 demonstrated the decidability of the word equation problem, which was later confirmed to be in PSPACE~\cite{10.1145/1132516.1132584 .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, even the leading SMT solvers with support for string constraints (including cvc5~\cite{10.1007/978-3-030-99524-9_24 , Z3~\cite{demoura2008z , Norn~\cite{10.1007/978-3-319-21690-4_29 , TRAU~\cite{8602997 , Ostrich~\cite{DBLP:journals/pacmpl/ChenHLRW19 , Woorpje~\cite{day2019solving , and Z3-Noodler~\cite{10.1007/978-3-031-57246-3_2 ) tend to be incomplete for proving the unsatisfiability of word equations, illustrating the hardness of the theory.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Solving a word equation is to check for the existence of string values for variables that make equal both sides of the equation.
elements.sent-2411-020: For example, consider the equation $Xab=YaZ$, where $X, Y$, and $Z$ are variables ranging over strings, and $a$ and $b$ are letters.
elements.sent-2411-021: This equation is satisfiable and has multiple solutions.
elements.sent-2411-022: For example, assigning $a$ to both $X$ and $Y$ and $b$ to $Z$ results in $aab=aab$.
elements.sent-2411-023: This paper presents an algorithm that makes use of Graph Neural Networks (GNN)~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-1806-01261 in order to solve word equations.}
elements.sent-2411-024: It is an extension of the method proposed in~\cite{10.1007/978-3-319-08867-9_10 and implemented in Norn~\cite{10.1007/978-3-319-21690-4_29 , referred to as the split algorithm.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: The split algorithm is, in turn, based on the well-known Nielsen transformation~\cite{Nielsen1917 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: It builds a proof tree by iteratively applying a set of inference (split) rules on a word equation.
elements.sent-2411-027: One critical aspect of the algorithm lies in selecting the next branch to be explored while constructing the proof tree, which significantly influences the solving time.
elements.sent-2411-028: To address this, we present a heuristic that leverages deep learning to determine the exploration order of branches.
elements.sent-2411-029: GNNs~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-1806-01261 represent one of the paradigms in neural network research, tailored for non-Euclidean, graph-structured data.}
elements.sent-2411-030: This makes them suited for scenarios where data points are interconnected, such as social networks~\cite{10.1145/3308558.3313488 , molecular structures~\cite{10.5555/3305381.3305512 , programs~\cite{10.5555/3015812.3016002,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-1711-00740 , and logical formulae~\cite{10.5555/3294996.3295038,10.1007/978-3-030-51054-1_29,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-1905-10006,LPAR2023:Guiding_an_Instantiation_Prover .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Our work represents, to the best of our knowledge, the first use of deep learning in the context of word equations.
elements.sent-2411-032: Figure~\ref{fig:workflow-diagram illustrates the workflow of our approach.}
elements.sent-2411-033: During the training stage, we initially employ the split algorithm (without GNN guidance) to solve word equation problems drawn from a training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-034: For each satisfiable (SAT) problem, we generate a corresponding proof tree.
elements.sent-2411-035: Within this tree, each branch (pair of a node and a direct child) is evaluated to determine whether it leads to a solution, as well as its distance to the corresponding leaf.
elements.sent-2411-036: Based on this information, we assign labels to each branch to indicate whether it is a favorable choice for reaching a solution.
elements.sent-2411-037: Subsequently, we encode each branching point comprising the node and its child nodes as a graph.
elements.sent-2411-038: These graph representations, along with their associated labels, are then used to train the GNN model.
elements.sent-2411-039: In the prediction stage, word equations from an evaluation dataset are processed using the split algorithm, now guided by the GNN model.
elements.sent-2411-040: At each branching point, the current branch is first transformed into a graph representation, which is in turn fed to the trained deep learning model with GNNs.
elements.sent-2411-041: The trained model, using its learned insights, advises on which branch should be prioritized and explored first.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth,trim=20 5 20 5]{figures/workflow-diagram.pdf} \end{center} \caption{The workflow diagram for the training and prediction stage} \label{fig:workflow-diagram}
elements.sent-2411-042: We implemented this algorithm in the \OURSOLVER tool.
elements.sent-2411-043: Experiments were conducted using four word equation benchmarks: three of them are artificially generated and inspired by Woorpje~\cite{day2019solving ; a fourth one is extracted from SMT-LIB benchmarks~\cite{smtlib:benchmark and encodes real-world problems.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Results show that for SAT problems, the pure split algorithm without GNNs is already competitive with some leading string solvers (Z3~\cite{demoura2008z , cvc5~\cite{10.1007/978-3-030-99524-9_24 , Ostrich~\cite{DBLP:journals/pacmpl/ChenHLRW19 , Woorpje~\cite{day2019solving , Z3-Noodler~\cite{10.1007/978-3-031-57246-3_2 ), while it performs less well on UNSAT problems.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: We conjecture that this is due to the (relatively straightforward) depth-first search performed by our implementation of the split algorithm, which is a good strategy for finding solutions, whereas other solvers devote more time (e.g., using length reasoning) to show that formulas are unsatisfiable.
elements.sent-2411-046: Enabling GNN guidance in \OURSOLVER uniformly improves performance on SAT problems, allowing it to outperform all other solvers in one specific benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-047: Specifically, in Benchmark~2, the GNN-guided version of \OURSOLVER solves 115\% more SAT problems than its non-GNN-guided counterpart and 43.0\% more than the next best string solver, Woorpje.
elements.sent-2411-048: In summary, the contributions of this paper are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-049: We define a proof system based on the split algorithm for solving word equations, tailored to combining symbolic reasoning with GNN-based guidance.
elements.sent-2411-050: Based on the proof system, we introduce an algorithm for integrating GNN-based guidance with the proof system.
elements.sent-2411-051: To train a GNN on the data obtained from solving word equations, we present five possible graph representations of word equations.
elements.sent-2411-052: We present an extensive experimental evaluation on four word equation benchmarks, comparing, in particular, the different graph encodings and different backtracking strategies of the algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-053: We start by defining the syntax of word equations, as well as the notion of satisfiability.
elements.sent-2411-054: Then, we explain the fundamental mechanism of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), along with a description of the specific GNN model we have employed in our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-055: We assume a finite non-empty alphabet $\Sigma$ and write $\Sigma^{*}$ for the set of all strings (or words) over $\Sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-056: We work with a set $\Gamma$ of string variables, ranging over words in $\Sigma^{*}$, and denote the empty string by $\epsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-057: The symbol $\cdot$ denotes the concatenation of two strings; in our examples, we often write~$uv$ as shorthand for $u \cdot v$.
elements.sent-2411-058: The syntax of word equations is defined as follows: \begin{equation}Formulae ~ \phi ::= true \mid e \land \phi Words ~ w ::= \epsilon \mid t \cdot w Equations ~ e ::= w = w Terms ~ t ::= X \mid c\end{equation} where $X \in \Gamma$ ranges over variables and $c \in \Sigma$ over letters.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}Formulae ~ \phi ::= true \mid e \land \phi Words ~ w ::= \epsilon \mid t \cdot w Equations ~ e ::= w = w Terms ~ t ::= X \mid c\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-059: A Graph Neural Network (GNN)~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-1806-01261 uses Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) to extract features from a given graph.}
elements.sent-2411-060: MLPs, also known as multi-layer neural networks~\cite{GoodBengCour16 , transform an input space to make different classes of data linearly separable, and this way learn representations of data with multiple levels of abstraction.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Each layer of an MLP consists of neurons that apply a nonlinear transformation to the inputs received from the previous layer.
elements.sent-2411-062: This allows MLPs to learn increasingly complex patterns as data moves from the input layer to the output layer.
elements.sent-2411-063: Message passing-based GNNs (MP-GNNs) \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/GilmerSRVD17 are designed to learn features of graph nodes (and potentially the entire graph) by iteratively aggregating and transforming feature information from the neighborhood of a node.}
elements.sent-2411-064: For instance, if we represent variables in a word equation by nodes in a graph, then node features could represent symbol type (i.e., being a variable), possible assignments, or the position in the word equation.
elements.sent-2411-065: Consider a graph $ G = (V, E) $, with $ V $ as the set of nodes and $ E \subseteq V \times V $ as the set of edges.
elements.sent-2411-066: Each node $ v \in V $ has an initial representation $ x_{v \in \mathbb{R ^{n}$ and a set of neighbors $ N_{v \subseteq V $.}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: In an MP-GNN comprising $ T $ message-passing steps, node representations are iteratively updated.
elements.sent-2411-068: At each step $ t $, the representation of node $ v $, denoted as $ h_{v ^{t $, is updated using the equation: \begin{equation}\label{eq:MPGNN} h_{v}^{t} = \eta_{t}(\rho_{t}(\{h_{u}^{t-1} \mid u \in N_{v}\}), h_{v}^{t-1}),\end{equation} where $ h_{v ^{t \in \mathbb{R ^{n $ is the updated representation of node $ v $ after $ t $ iterations, starting from the initial representation $ h_{v ^{0 = x_{v $.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: The node representation of $u$ in the previous iteration $t-1$ is $h_{u ^{t-1}$, and node $u$ is a neighbor of node $v$.}
elements.sent-2411-070: In this context, $\rho_{t :(\mathbb{R ^{n )^{|N_{v | \rightarrow \mathbb{R ^{n}$ is an aggregation function with trainable parameters (e.g., an MLP followed by sum, mean, min, or max) that aggregates the node representations of $v$'s neighboring nodes at the $t$-th iteration.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: Along with this, $\eta_{t :(\mathbb{R ^{n )^2\rightarrow \mathbb{R ^{n}$ is an update function with trainable parameters (e.g., an MLP) that takes the aggregated node representation from $\rho_{t}$ and the node representation of $v$ in the previous iteration as input, and outputs the updated node representation of $v$ at the $t$-th iteration.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: MP-GNNs operate under the assumption that node features can capture structural information from long-distance neighbors by aggregating and updating features of neighboring nodes.
elements.sent-2411-073: After $T$ message-passing steps, an MP-GNN yields an updated node representation (feature) that includes information from neighbors within a distance of $T$, applicable to various downstream tasks like node or graph classification.
elements.sent-2411-074: In this study, we choose Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs)~\cite{kipf2017semisupervised to guide our algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-075: In GCNs, the node representation $h_{v ^{t}$ of $v$ at step $t \in \{1, ..., T\}$ where $T \in \mathbb{N}$ is computed by \begin{equation}\label{eq:GCN} h_{v}^{t} = \text{ReLU}(\text{MLP}^{t}(\text{mean}\{h_{u}^{t-1} \mid u \in N_{v} \cup \{v\}\})),\end{equation} where each $\text{MLP ^{t}$ is a fully connected neural network, ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit)~\cite{agarap2018deep is the non-linear function $f(x)=max(0,x)$, and $h_{v ^{0 =x_{v}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: In this section, we define our proof system for word equations, the notion of a proof tree, and show soundness and completeness.
elements.sent-2411-077: We then introduce an algorithm to solve a conjunction of word equations.
elements.sent-2411-078: We introduce four types of proof rules in Figure~\ref{fig:split_rules , each corresponding to a specific situation.}
elements.sent-2411-079: The proof rules are inspired by~\cite{10.1007/978-3-319-08867-9_10 , but streamlined and formulated differently.}

elements.sent-2411-082: In our case, $P$ is a conjunction of word equations and each $C_i$ is either a conjunction of word equations or a final state, \SAT or \UNSAT.

elements.sent-2411-088: Rule $R_{7}$ (Figure~\ref{fig:rules_Rvc ) manages cases where one side begins with a letter and the other one with a variable.}

elements.sent-2411-093: Although our proof system is not complete for proving the unsatisfiability of word equations, we can observe that the proof rules are sound and locally complete.
elements.sent-2411-094: A proof rule is said to be sound if the satisfiability of the premise implies the satisfiability of one of the conclusions.
elements.sent-2411-095: It is said to be locally complete if the satisfiability of one of the conclusions implies the satisfiability of the premise.
elements.sent-2411-096: The proof rules in Figure~\ref{fig:split_rules are sound and locally complete.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Iteratively applying the proof rules to a conjunction of word equations gives rise to a proof tree growing downwards.
elements.sent-2411-098: Given the proof rules $R_{1 ,\ldots,R_{9}$ in Figure~\ref{fig:split_rules , we represent a proof tree as a tuple $\tau = (N,\alpha,E,\lambda)$ where:.}}
elements.sent-2411-099: An edge $(n_{i ,n_{j )\in E$ implies that $n_{j}$ is derived from $n_{i}$ by applying a proof rule;.}}
elements.sent-2411-100: A path in the proof tree is a sequence of edges starting from the root and ending with a leaf node.
elements.sent-2411-101: Due to local completeness, if there is a leaf node that is \SAT, then the word equation at the root node is satisfiable.
elements.sent-2411-102: Due to soundness, if all the leaf nodes are \UNSAT, then the formula at the root node is unsatisfiable.
elements.sent-2411-103: Figure~\ref{figure:proof-tree illustrates the proof tree generated by applying the proof rules in Figure~\ref{fig:split_rules on the word equation $\phi = (XbY = bXXZ)$.}}

elements.sent-2411-131: Graph representations can capture the structural information in word equations and are the standard input format for GNNs.

elements.sent-2411-133: In order to extract a single graph from the equations, we first translate a conjunction~$\bigwedge_{i=1 ^n w_{i ^{l = w_{i ^{r}$ of word equations to a single word equation, by inserting a distinguished letter $\# \notin \Sigma$ as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:sharp-encoding} w_{1}^{l}\# w_{2}^{l} \# ... \# w_{n}^{l}= w_{1}^{r}\# w_{2}^{r} \# ... \# w_{n}^{r},\end{equation} Then, we construct the graph representations for the word equation in \eqref{eq:sharp-encoding .}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-134: A graph representation $G=(V,E,V_{\text{T ,V_{\text{Var )$ of a word equation consists of a set of nodes $V$, a set of edges $E \subseteq V \times V$, a set of terminal nodes $V_{\text{T }\subseteq V$, and a set of variable nodes $V_{\text{Var \subseteq V$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-135: We start constructing the graph by drawing the ``$=$'' symbol as the root node.
elements.sent-2411-136: Its left and right children are the leftmost terms of both sides of the equation, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-137: The rest of the graph is built following the choice of the graph type:.
elements.sent-2411-138: Graph 1: Inspired by Abstract Syntax Trees (ASTs).
elements.sent-2411-139: Each letter and variable is represented by its own node, and words are represented by singly-linked lists of nodes.
elements.sent-2411-140: Graph 2: An extension of Graph 1, introducing additional edges from each term node back to the root node.
elements.sent-2411-141: Graph 3: An extension of Graph 1 which incorporates unique variable nodes.
elements.sent-2411-142: In this design, nodes representing variables are added, which are connected to their respective occurrences in the linked lists.
elements.sent-2411-143: This representation aims at facilitating the learning of long-distance variable relationships by GNNs.
elements.sent-2411-144: Graph 4: Similar in approach to Graph 3, but introducing unique nodes for letters instead of variables.
elements.sent-2411-145: Graph 5: A composite structure that merges the concepts of Graphs 3 and 4.
elements.sent-2411-146: It includes unique nodes for both variables and letters..

elements.sent-2411-171: Each node~$v$ of the proof tree with multiple children is labelled based on two criteria: the satisfiability status (\SAT, \UNSAT, or \UNKNOWN) of the formula, and the size of the proof sub-tree underneath each of the direct children.

elements.sent-2411-174: If multiple children are \SAT, we examine the size of the sub-tree underneath each of those children, and label all children with minimal sub-trees with $1$ in the list~$(x_1, \ldots, x_n)$.

elements.subsection-2411-007: Guidance for the Split Algorithm using the GNN Model
elements.sent-2411-177: In Algorithm~\ref{algorithm:SolveEqs , we introduce five strategies for the orderBranches function implementation, designed to evaluate the efficiency of deterministic versus stochastic methods in branch ordering and to investigate the interplay between fixed and variable branch ordering approaches:.}
elements.sent-2411-178: Fixed Order: Use a predetermined branch order, defined before execution.
elements.sent-2411-179: In our experiments, we simply use the order in which the branches are displayed in Figure~\ref{fig:split_rules .}
elements.sent-2411-181: GNN (S1): Exclusively use the GNN model for branch ordering.
elements.sent-2411-182: GNN-fixed (S2): A balanced approach with a 50\% chance of using the GNN model and a 50\% chance of using the fixed order.
elements.sent-2411-183: GNN-random (S3): Similar to S2, but with the alternative 50\% chance dedicated to random ordering.
elements.sent-2411-184: This section presents the benchmarks used for our experiments and details the results with the different versions of our algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-185: It also provides a comprehensive comparison with other state-of-the-art solvers.
elements.sent-2411-186: We train the models with PyTorch~\cite{NEURIPS2019_9015 and construct the GNNs using the Deep Graph Library (DGL)~\cite{wang2019dgl .}}
elements.sent-2411-187: For tracking and visualizing training experiments, mlflow~\cite{10.1145/3399579.3399867 is employed.}
elements.sent-2411-188: Proof trees and graph representations of word equations are stored in JSON~\cite{pezoa2016foundations format, while graphviz~\cite{Ellson2004 is utilized for their tracking and visualization.}}
elements.sent-2411-189: We consider two kinds of benchmarks: benchmarks that are artificially generated based on the benchmarks used to evaluate the solver Woorpje~\cite{day2019solving , as well as benchmarks extracted from the non-incremental QF\_S, QF\_SLIA, and QF\_SNLIA track of the SMT-LIB benchmarks~\cite{smtlib:benchmark .}}
elements.sent-2411-190: We summarize the benchmarks as following:.
elements.sent-2411-191: Benchmark 1 is generated by the mechanism used in Woorpje track~I.
elements.sent-2411-192: Given finite sets of letters~$C$ and variables~$V$, we construct a string $s$ with maximum length of $k$ by randomly concatenating selected letters from \(C\).
elements.sent-2411-193: We then form a word equation $s = s$ and repeatedly replace substrings in $s$ with the concatenation of between $1$ and $5$ fresh variables.
elements.sent-2411-194: This procedure guarantees that the constructed word equation is \SAT.
elements.sent-2411-195: Benchmark 2 is generated by the mechanism used in Woorpje track~III.
elements.sent-2411-196: It first generates a word equation using the following definition: \begin{equation}\label{eq:track02} X_{n}aX_{n}bX_{n-1}bX_{n-2}\cdots bX_{1} = aX_{n}X_{n-1}X_{n-1}bX_{n-2}X_{n-2}b \cdots b X_{1}X_{1}baa\end{equation} where $X_{1 , ..., X_{n}$ are variables and $a$ and $b$ are letters.}
elements.sent-2411-197: We then generate a word equation using the mechanism for Benchmark~1, and replace letters~$b$ in \eqref{eq:track02 randomly with the left-hand side or the right-hand side of that equation.}
elements.sent-2411-198: Benchmark 3 is generated by conjoining multiple word equations that were randomly generated using the mechanism described in Benchmark 1.
elements.sent-2411-199: This procedure mainly produces benchmarks that are \UNSAT.
elements.sent-2411-200: Benchmark 4 is extracted from benchmarks from the non-incremental QF\_S, QF\_SLIA, and QF\_SNLIA tracks of SMT-LIB.
elements.sent-2411-201: We obtain word equations by removing length constraints, regular expressions, and unsupported Boolean operators, which are not considered in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-202: As a result, benchmarks after transformation can be \SAT even if the original SMT-LIB benchmarks were \UNSAT.

elements.sent-2411-212: We measured the number of solved problems and the average solving time (in seconds), with timeout of 300 seconds for each proof attempt.

elements.sent-2411-214: The performance of the GNN-guided \OURSOLVER (row GNN in Table~\ref{tbl:evaluation-table-1 ) for each benchmark is selected from the best results out of 45 experiments (see Table~\ref{tbl:evaluation-table-2 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-215: These experiments use different combinations of five graph representations, three backtrack strategies, and three GNN guidance strategies, as shown in bold text in Table~\ref{tbl:evaluation-table-2 .}
elements.sent-2411-216: We compare the results with those of five other solvers: Z3 (v4.12.2)~\cite{demoura2008z , Z3-Noodler (v1.1.0)~\cite{10.1007/978-3-031-57246-3_2 , cvc5 (v1.0.8)~\cite{10.1007/978-3-030-99524-9_24 , Ostrich (v1.3)~\cite{DBLP:journals/pacmpl/ChenHLRW19 , and Woopje (v0.2)~\cite{day2019solving .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-217: The primary metric is the number of solved problems.
elements.sent-2411-218: Notably, the GNN-based \OURSOLVER solves the highest number of \SAT problems.
elements.sent-2411-219: For the conjunction of multiple word equations (benchmark 3 and 4), \OURSOLVER's performance is comparable to the other solvers.
elements.sent-2411-220: The order of processing word equations is crucial for those problems; currently, our solver uses only a predefined sequence, indicating significant potential for improvement.
elements.sent-2411-221: A limitation of \OURSOLVER is determining \UNSAT cases, as it requires an exhaustive check of all nodes in the proof tree.
elements.sent-2411-222: In terms of average solving time for solved problems, GNN-based \OURSOLVER does not hold an advantage.
elements.sent-2411-223: This is mainly due to the overhead associated with encoding equations into a graph at each split point and invoking GNNs.
elements.sent-2411-224: Furthermore, \OURSOLVER is written in Python and not particularly optimized at this point, so that there is ample room for improvement in future efforts.
elements.sent-2411-225: The measurement of the average number of splits in solved problems is used to gain insight into the efficiency of the different versions of our algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-226: For benchmark 1 and 2, the GNN-guided version outperforms the others on the commonly solved problems.
elements.sent-2411-227: However, for benchmarks 3 and 4, the GNN-guided version does not show advantages.
elements.sent-2411-228: This is for the same reason mentioned in the metric of the number of solved problems; namely, the performance is also influenced by the order of processing equations when dealing with the conjunction of multiple word equations.
elements.sent-2411-229: We summarize the experimental results compared with some of the leading string solvers as follows:.

elements.sent-2411-231: In contrast, other solvers may invest more time in proving UNSAT cases, for instance by reasoning about string length or Parikh vectors.
elements.sent-2411-232: This performance difference is because the initial choice of word equation for splitting is crucial for the split algorithm, and this aspect is not optimized currently.
elements.sent-2411-233: Incorporating GNN guidance into the proof tree search enhances the performance of the pure split algorithm for \SAT problems, but currently does not lead to an improvement for \UNSAT problems.
elements.sent-2411-234: Table~\ref{tbl:evaluation-table-2 displays the number of problems solved in 45 experiments across all benchmarks using the GNN-guided version.}
elements.sent-2411-235: In terms of backtrack strategies, $BT_1$ performs a pure depth-first search, but it already has good performance.
elements.sent-2411-236: This indicates that more sophisticated search strategies may lead to even better performance.
elements.sent-2411-237: In terms of the guiding strategies (S1, S2, S3), using the GNN alone (S1) to guide the branch order is better than combining it with predefined and random orders (S2 and S3) in most cases.
elements.sent-2411-238: This indicates that the GNN model successfully learns useful patterns at each split point and can be used as a stand-alone heuristic for branching.
elements.sent-2411-239: In terms of the five graph representations, Graph 1 has the simplest structure, which represents the syntactic information of the word equations and thus incurs the least overhead when we call the model at each split point.
elements.sent-2411-240: This yields average performance compared to other graph representations.
elements.sent-2411-241: The performances of Graph 2 are weaker than others; this is probably due to the extra edges not providing any benefits for prediction, but leading to additional computational overhead.
elements.sent-2411-242: Graphs 3 and 4 emphasize the relationships between terminals and variables, respectively, thus the performance is biased by individual problems.
elements.sent-2411-243: Graph 5 considers the relationships for both terminals and variables, thus it has bigger overhead than Graphs 1, 3, and 4, but it offers relatively good performance.
elements.sent-2411-244: This shows that representing semantic information of the word equations well in graphs helps the model to learn important patterns.
elements.sent-2411-245: In summary, the setting $(BT_{2 , S1, Graph5)$ performs the best.}
elements.sent-2411-246: There are many techniques within solvers supporting word equations, as well as in stand-alone word equation solvers.
elements.sent-2411-247: For instance, the SMT solvers Norn~\cite{10.1007/978-3-319-21690-4_29 and TRAU~\cite{8602997 introduce several improvements on the inference rules~\cite{10.1007/978-3-319-08867-9_10 , including length-guided splitting of equalities and a more efficient way to handle disequalities.}}}
elements.sent-2411-248: The stand-alone word equation solver Woorpje~\cite{day2019solving reformulates the word equation problem as a reachability problem for nondeterministic finite automata, then encodes it as a propositional satisfiability problem which can be handled by SAT solvers.}
elements.sent-2411-249: In~\cite{10.1145/3372020.3391556 , the authors propose a transformation system that extends the Nielsen transformation~\cite{levi1944semigroups to work with linear length constraints.}}
elements.sent-2411-250: This transformation system can be integrated into existing string solvers such as Z3STR3~\cite{8102241 , Z3SEQ~\cite{demoura2008z , and CVC4~\cite{10.1007/978-3-642-22110-1_14 , thereby advancing the efficiency of word equation resolution.}}}
elements.sent-2411-251: GNNs excel at analyzing the graph-like structures of logic formulae, offering a complementary approach to formal verification.
elements.sent-2411-252: FormulaNet~\cite{10.5555/3294996.3295038 is an early study guiding the premise selection for Automated Theorem Provers (ATPs).}
elements.sent-2411-253: It uses MP-GNNs~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/GilmerSRVD17 to process the graph representation of the formulae in the proof trace extracted from HOL Light~\cite{10.1007/978-3-642-03359-9_4 .}}
elements.sent-2411-254: With more studies~\cite{10.1007/978-3-030-51054-1_29,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-1905-10006,LPAR2023:Guiding_an_Instantiation_Prover exploring this path, this trend quickly expands to related fields.}
elements.sent-2411-255: For instance, for SAT solvers~\cite{wang2023neuroback,kurin2020improving , NeuroSAT~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-1903-04671,selsam2019learning predicts the probability of variables appearing in unsat cores to guide the variable branching decisions for Conflict-Driven Clause Learning (CDCL)~\cite{MarquesSilva1999GRASPAS .}}}
elements.sent-2411-256: Moreover, GNNs have been combined with various formal verification techniques, such as scheduling SMT \mbox{solvers ~\cite{9643296 , loop invariant reasoning~\cite{NEURIPS201865b1e92c,10.1007/978-3-030-53291-8-9 , or guiding Constraint Horn Clause (CHC) \mbox{solvers ~\cite{10.1007/978-3-031-50524-9_13,liang2022exploring,8603013 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-257: They provide the empirical foundations for designing the learning task in Section~\ref{section:learning , such as the graph representation of word equations and forming the learning task in split points.}
elements.sent-2411-258: This study introduces a GNN-guided split algorithm for solving word equations, along with five graph representations to enhance branch ordering through a multi-classification task at each split point of the proof tree.
elements.sent-2411-259: We developed our solver from scratch instead of modifying a state-of-the-art SMT solver.
elements.sent-2411-260: This decision prevents the confounding influences of pre-existing optimizations in state-of-the-art SMT solvers, allowing us to isolate and evaluate the specific impact of GNN guidance more effectively.
elements.sent-2411-261: We investigate various configurations, including graph representations, backtrack strategies, and the conditions for employing GNN-guided branches, aiming to analyze the behaviors of the algorithm across different settings.
elements.sent-2411-262: The evaluation tables reveal that while the split algorithm effectively solves single word equations, it does not demonstrate marked improvements for multiple conjunctive word equations relative to other solvers.
elements.sent-2411-263: This discrepancy is attributed to the significance of the processing order for conjunctive word equations, where our current solver employs a predefined order.
elements.sent-2411-264: It is possible to make use of a GNN to compute the best equation to start with.
elements.sent-2411-265: However, this involves ranking a list of elements with variable lengths, rather than performing a fixed-category classification task, and requires completely different training for this specific task.
elements.sent-2411-266: Consequently, as future work, we aim to investigate both deterministic and stochastic strategies to optimize the ordering of conjunctive word equations for the split algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-267: Our algorithm is also limited in handling ~~UNSAT problems because it can only conclude UNSAT by exhausting the proof tree.
